content.rendered:
Barrett Strong’s original 1959 recording of “Money (That’s What I Want)” ushered in the age of Motown as a cultural force. Peaking at #2 on the Billboard R&B chart and #23 on the Hot 100, “Money” was Motown’s first of many hit singles.

If the original recording below sounds a bit loose, that’s because it was: “Money” was the result of an improvisational recording session–so improvisational in fact, that the guitarist and bassist remained unknown for years, because they were high school amateurs who happened to be passing by the studio at the right time.

Just a few years later, of course, The Beatles would cover Strong’s hit single, bringing the song to an even wider audience.

The Beatles’ version is probably the one that first captured Bruce’s attention; his high school band The Castiles began covering it in 1965, barely a year after the song appeared on With the Beatles.

We know The Castiles covered it thanks to surviving set list documents, but unfortunately there are no surviving recordings. (It’s amazing that anything survives from the Castiles era at all. Who could have predicted Bruce’s rise to fame back then?)

In fact, even though Bruce has covered “Money (That’s What I Want)” at least a half-dozen times over the years, it’s maddeningly difficult to track down audio evidence of it, since none of those times were during an “official” concert, E Street or otherwise.

But we do have two vintage recordings of Bruce covering this R&B classic, both dating back to surprise Jersey shore club appearances in the 1980s.

The first was with Beaver Brown at the short-lived Big Man’s West in February 1982. The recording is muddy, but it’s clear enough to tell just how hot a performance it was on what must have been a very cold night:

The next time Bruce covered “Money” was more than five years later–this time at The Stone Pony in Asbury Park in the summer of 1987, during a rare full E Street Band appearance (almost, anyway–Nils wasn’t there that night) at the legendary club. This one’s a bit clearer:

Updated 1/7/2021: Bruce has played “Money (That’s What I Want)” a couple of times since, but you’d have had to be fortunate, connected or wealthy enough to attend the private benefit shows where he performed them, like this 2016 benefit for the U.S. Equestrian Team.

The rest of us will have to be content with these rare club recordings from the eighties.

Money (That’s What I Want)
First performed: February 20, 1982 (Red Bank, NJ)
Last performed: January 22, 2016 (Wellington, FL)

November 26, 2019

excerpt.rendered: Bruce Springsteen has covered Motown’s first big hit several times over the years, but only two club performances from the eighties were caught on tape. Take a listen to both inside.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Everybody’s Trying to Be My Baby
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: One time only: Bruce joins Steve Earle on stage for a cover of a rock classic that goes much further back than you might think.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Slow Down
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: One time only: Bruce covers Larry Williams' influential "Slow Down" with a cover band at the Classics Cafe in Westwood, New Jersey.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Cover Me: No Money Down
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: You've heard snippets in River-era bootlegs, but if you haven't heard the one and only recording of Bruce's full cover of "No Money Down" from way back in 1974, you need to listen to this definitive version inside.

jetpack-related-posts.title: I Wanna Be Your Man
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: It was a surprising choice for a surprise performance, but it proved so nice they did it twice.

jetpack-related-posts.title: I Saw Her Standing There
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Rock royalty gathers in 1988 to induct The Beatles into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame, and Bruce takes a turn at the mic on their 1963 classic.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Hippy Hippy Shake
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: From the Legends of Springsteen files: Bruce and Max join cover band Stand and Deliver at the Classics Cafe in Westwood for rocking rendition of "Hippy Hippy Shake."
